question: Explain the concept of wireframing and its importance in UI/UX design process.
expectedPoints: Wireframe is a visual skeleton showing layout structure without colors/images
Serves as blueprint for digital products
Allows early validation of usability concepts
Enables communication with stakeholders before visual design
Cost-effective way to iterate on layout structure

question: Differentiate between low-fidelity, mid-fidelity, and high-fidelity wireframes with examples of when each should be used.
expectedPoints: Low-fidelity: simple sketches, basic shapes, for rapid concept validation
Mid-fidelity: digital wireframes with proper spacing, for refined layouts
High-fidelity: detailed with placeholder content and basic interactivity, for near-final review
Use Lo-Fi for early brainstorming
Use Mid-Fi for stakeholder presentations
Use Hi-Fi for detailed layout testing

question: List and explain five essential elements that should be included in a website wireframe.
expectedPoints: Header with logo and navigation
Search functionality
Content area layout
Call-to-action buttons
Forms and input fields
Footer with links
Sidebar elements if applicable
Each element serves specific user needs

question: What is the difference between a wireframe, a mockup, and a prototype? Explain with examples.
expectedPoints: Wireframe: skeletal structure, grayscale, shows layout only
Mockup: static visual design with colors, typography, images
Prototype: interactive model simulating user flows and functionality
Wireframe → Mockup → Prototype is the typical progression
Each serves different purpose in design process

question: Explain the 10-foot interface rule and its significance in wireframe design.
expectedPoints: Key elements should be visible from 10 feet away
Important for quick scanning and accessibility
Applies to CTAs, headlines, navigation
Ensures critical information stands out
Helps prioritize content hierarchy in wireframes

question: Describe the benefits of wireframing in the design development cycle.
expectedPoints: Early validation of UX concepts before development
Cost-effective iteration - changes are cheaper
Clear communication with stakeholders and developers
Identifies usability issues early
Faster development handoff with clear specifications
Facilitates user testing at low cost

question: Explain what responsive wireframing is and why it is important for modern web design.
expectedPoints: Creating wireframes that adapt to different screen sizes
Ensures layout works on mobile, tablet, and desktop
Mobile-first approach is recommended
Tests information hierarchy across devices
Important due to diverse device usage patterns
Helps identify content prioritization for smaller screens

question: Discuss the wireframing process from planning to validation. Include steps, tools, and best practices.
expectedPoints: Step 1: Define objectives and user requirements
Step 2: Research and gather user insights
Step 3: Create low-fidelity sketches for initial concepts
Step 4: Develop mid-fidelity digital wireframes
Step 5: Add annotations for functionality
Step 6: Review with stakeholders
Step 7: Test with users
Step 8: Iterate based on feedback
Step 9: Develop high-fidelity wireframes
Step 10: Prepare for visual design handoff
Tools: Figma, Adobe XD, Balsamiq, Sketch
Best practices: Keep it simple, use consistent components, follow naming conventions

question: Compare popular wireframing tools (Figma, Balsamiq, Adobe XD) and recommend when to use each.
expectedPoints: Figma: Browser-based, collaborative, great for hi-fi wireframes and prototypes, free tier available
Balsamiq: Hand-drawn style, perfect for lo-fi brainstorming, simple learning curve
Adobe XD: Vector-based, good for interactive prototypes, part of Adobe ecosystem
Sketch: Mac-only, extensive plugin ecosystem, industry standard for many
Consider factors: team collaboration, fidelity needed, budget, learning curve
Figma recommended for teams needing collaboration
Balsamiq for quick concept exploration
Adobe XD for Adobe workflow users
Each has unique strengths depending on project needs

question: Design a wireframe for an e-commerce product page. Include all necessary elements and explain your layout decisions.
expectedPoints: Header: Logo, search bar, navigation menu, cart icon, user account
Product image area (main image with thumbnails)
Product title and price display
Rating/review summary
Size/variant selectors
Quantity selector
Add to Cart / Buy Now buttons
Product description accordion
Related products section
Footer with policies and links
Layout rationale: F-pattern for scanning, prominent CTAs, easy access to cart, trust elements
Mobile considerations: stacked layout, prominent touch targets
Annotations for interactivity
